meta.title: Data Engineering
meta.subtitle: build reliable data pipelines
meta.blurb: From ingestion and storage to transformation, orchestration, and serving — the infrastructure that turns raw data into trusted, queryable assets.

t-de-intro.label: What is Data Engineering?
t-de-intro.desc: The discipline of designing, building, and maintaining systems that collect, store, and transform data for analysis and ML.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data engineering is the **infrastructure layer** that makes data usable. You build pipelines that move data from sources to destinations, transforming it along the way so analysts, scientists, and ML systems can trust and query it.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: The lifecycle: **generate → ingest → store → transform → serve**. A data engineer owns reliability, scalability, and cost efficiency across this chain. Adjacent concerns: security, privacy, data management, and DataOps.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026 the role blends software engineering with data infrastructure. You're expected to write production-quality code, understand distributed systems, operate cloud services, and collaborate closely with analytics and ML teams. The stack is converging around SQL + Python + cloud-native managed services.

t-data-architectures.label: Data Architecture Patterns
t-data-architectures.desc: Warehouse, lake, lakehouse, mesh — the major architectural paradigms and when to use each.

links.title: Lakehouse: A New Generation of Open Platforms (Databricks)

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Data warehouse**: structured, schema-on-write, optimised for BI (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift). **Data lake**: raw files in object storage, schema-on-read, cheap and flexible. **Lakehouse**: combines both — open file formats on a lake with warehouse-like performance and transactions.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Data mesh** decentralises ownership: domain teams own their data as products, with federated governance. It's an organisational pattern, not a technology. In practice, most teams adopt a **lakehouse** with domain-oriented ownership principles from mesh.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: By 2026 the lakehouse is the dominant new-build architecture: open formats (Parquet/Iceberg v3) on object storage, query engines (Spark, Trino, Flink 2.2, DuckDB) on top, and a catalog (**Apache Polaris**, Unity Catalog) for governance. Pure warehouses still dominate BI; pure lakes still power ML feature pipelines.

t-batch-vs-stream.label: Batch vs Streaming
t-batch-vs-stream.desc: Two fundamental processing paradigms — processing data in large chunks vs processing it continuously as it arrives.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Batch**: process accumulated data on a schedule (hourly, daily). Simple, efficient, high throughput. **Streaming**: process events as they arrive, with seconds-level latency. More complex but enables real-time use cases.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Lambda architecture** runs both batch and stream in parallel (complex but complete). **Kappa architecture** uses only streaming, replaying the log for reprocessing. In practice, most systems are batch-first with streaming for the few use cases that truly need low latency.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, the boundary blurs: tools like **Apache Flink** and **Spark Structured Streaming** unify batch and stream with the same API. Default to batch (simpler, cheaper, easier to debug); add streaming only where latency requirements demand it (fraud detection, real-time recommendations, alerting).

t-data-modeling.label: Data Modeling
t-data-modeling.desc: Structuring data for efficient querying — dimensional modeling, normalisation, and schema design for analytics.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data modeling defines how data is **structured, related, and stored** for efficient access. For analytics, the dominant pattern is **dimensional modeling**: **fact tables** (events/metrics) surrounded by **dimension tables** (who, what, when, where).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Star schema**: denormalised dimensions for fast queries. **Snowflake schema**: normalised dimensions (saves space, adds joins). **Data Vault**: a historised, audit-friendly approach for enterprise data warehouses. **One Big Table (OBT)**: pre-joined wide tables for simple BI.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Kimball's dimensional modeling remains the standard for BI/analytics. Data Vault suits complex enterprise environments with many sources. With modern columnar engines (BigQuery, Snowflake), over-normalisation matters less — compute is cheap, modeler time is expensive. Use **dbt** to codify and version your models.

t-data-lakes.label: Data Lakes
t-data-lakes.desc: Centralised repositories of raw data stored in open formats on cheap object storage.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **data lake** stores all your raw data — structured, semi-structured, unstructured — in object storage (S3, GCS, ADLS). Cheap, durable, and schema-agnostic. You decide structure at read time.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Organise into zones: **raw/bronze** (as-is from source), **cleaned/silver** (validated, deduplicated), **curated/gold** (business-ready aggregates). **Partition** by date or key columns for efficient scans. A **catalog** (Glue, Hive Metastore) tracks schemas.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Without governance, lakes become swamps. Enforce naming conventions, access controls, and data quality checks per zone. Use table formats (Iceberg, Delta) to add ACID transactions, time-travel, and schema evolution on top of raw object storage.

t-file-formats.label: File Formats
t-file-formats.desc: Choosing the right serialisation format — Parquet, Avro, ORC, JSON, and their trade-offs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Columnar** formats (Parquet, ORC) store data by column — ideal for analytics (read only the columns you need, great compression). **Row** formats (Avro, JSON, CSV) store data by record — better for write-heavy or streaming workloads.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Parquet**: the standard for analytics lakes (columnar, compressed, splittable). **Avro**: schema-embedded, compact, ideal for Kafka and data interchange. **ORC**: Hive-optimised columnar. JSON/CSV: human-readable but large and slow at scale.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Default to **Parquet** for anything analytical. Use **Avro** for streaming/message serialisation (compact, schema evolution). Compression: Snappy for speed, Zstd for ratio. Row-group sizing and column statistics in Parquet directly impact query performance — tune them for your query patterns.

t-table-formats.label: Open Table Formats
t-table-formats.desc: Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, Hudi — adding warehouse features (ACID, time-travel, schema evolution) to data lakes.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Open table formats sit between your files (Parquet) and your query engine. They add **ACID transactions**, **time-travel** (query historical snapshots), **schema evolution**, and **partition evolution** to plain data lake files.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Iceberg**: engine-agnostic, hidden partitioning, strong community (Netflix origin). **Delta Lake**: Spark-native, Databricks ecosystem, log-based transactions. **Hudi**: Uber origin, strong for upserts/CDC. All three are converging in features.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: By 2026, **Iceberg** is the industry default for new projects — **Iceberg v3** reaching GA across major engines, with broad support from Spark, Trino, Flink 2.2, DuckDB, BigQuery, and Snowflake. **Apache Polaris** (graduated catalog) provides vendor-neutral governance. Delta Lake 4.0 (September 2025) added coordinated commits for multi-engine writes. Choose one and stick with it; the interoperability story (UniForm, XTable) is improving but still maturing.

t-warehouses.label: Cloud Data Warehouses
t-warehouses.desc: Managed analytical databases — Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift — optimised for complex queries over large datasets.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud warehouses store and query structured/semi-structured data at scale. **Separation of storage and compute** means you scale each independently and pay for what you use.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Snowflake**: multi-cloud, virtual warehouses, strong data sharing. **BigQuery**: serverless (no cluster management), slot-based pricing, great for Google ecosystem. **Redshift**: tightly integrated with AWS, Redshift Serverless simplifies ops. All support semi-structured data (JSON) natively.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Warehouses excel at BI and ad-hoc analytics. For cost control: use auto-suspend, right-size compute, cluster keys/sort keys, and materialised views. The trend is convergence with lakes — Snowflake reads Iceberg, BigQuery reads object storage, blurring the warehouse/lakehouse boundary.

t-batch-ingestion.label: Batch Ingestion
t-batch-ingestion.desc: Moving data in scheduled bulk loads — from databases, files, APIs, and SaaS platforms into your lake or warehouse.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Batch ingestion extracts data from sources on a schedule and loads it into your lake or warehouse. Two approaches: **full load** (copy everything) or **incremental** (only new/changed records since last run).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **ETL** (transform before loading) vs **ELT** (load raw, transform in-warehouse). ELT dominates in 2026 — cheap storage + powerful warehouse compute makes it simpler to land raw and transform with SQL/dbt. Tools: Fivetran, Airbyte, custom Spark/Python jobs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Incremental ingestion is critical at scale — full loads don't scale past medium datasets. Track watermarks (timestamps, sequence IDs) reliably. Handle schema drift from sources gracefully. Managed connectors (Fivetran, Airbyte) save huge effort for common SaaS sources; custom code for anything bespoke.

t-stream-ingestion.label: Stream Ingestion
t-stream-ingestion.desc: Capturing events in real-time — Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub — for continuous data pipelines.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Stream ingestion captures events **as they happen** into a durable log (Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub). Consumers read from the log in real-time or replay from any point in history.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Kafka** is the de facto standard: partitioned, replicated, ordered logs with configurable retention. **Kinesis** (AWS-native, simpler). **Pub/Sub** (GCP, serverless). Key concepts: partitions for parallelism, consumer groups for scaling, exactly-once semantics.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use managed Kafka (MSK, Confluent Cloud) unless you have a dedicated platform team. Partition by entity key for ordering guarantees. Schema Registry (Avro/Protobuf) prevents producer/consumer contract breakage. For most analytics, land streaming data into the lake in micro-batches (5–15 min) rather than true per-event processing.

t-cdc.label: Change Data Capture (CDC)
t-cdc.desc: Capturing row-level changes from databases in real-time — the bridge between OLTP and analytics.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **CDC** captures every insert, update, and delete from a source database and streams them downstream. This keeps your lake/warehouse in near-real-time sync without expensive full-table scans.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Log-based CDC** (Debezium, AWS DMS) reads the database's transaction log (WAL/binlog) — zero impact on source performance. Alternatives: trigger-based (older, invasive) and timestamp-based (misses deletes). Events land in Kafka, then flow to the lake.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: CDC is the gold standard for replicating operational databases into analytics. Debezium + Kafka + Iceberg/Delta is a proven stack. Handle schema changes carefully (add columns gracefully, never break downstream). For simple use cases, managed services (AWS DMS, Fivetran CDC mode) reduce operational burden significantly.

t-api-ingestion.label: API & SaaS Ingestion
t-api-ingestion.desc: Pulling data from REST APIs, webhooks, and SaaS platforms into your data platform.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Many data sources are only accessible via **REST APIs** (Stripe, Salesforce, GitHub, ad platforms). Ingestion means paginating through endpoints, handling rate limits, and landing the JSON in your lake.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Use managed connectors (Fivetran, Airbyte) for common SaaS — they handle pagination, auth, rate limits, and schema detection. For custom APIs, write Python extractors with retry logic, idempotent checkpointing, and incremental cursor tracking.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: API ingestion is deceptively complex: rate limits, inconsistent pagination, schema changes without notice, and partial failures. **Webhooks** (push-based) are faster than polling when available. Always store raw JSON in the lake before parsing — you can reprocess if your parsing logic changes.

t-sql-transforms.label: SQL Transformations
t-sql-transforms.desc: Using SQL as the primary transformation language — CTEs, window functions, and analytical patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: SQL is the **lingua franca** of data transformation. Most warehouse/lakehouse transformations are pure SQL: SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY, filtered and materialised into new tables or views.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **CTEs** (`WITH` clauses) for readable multi-step logic. **Window functions** (`ROW_NUMBER`, `LAG`, `SUM OVER`) for analytics without collapsing rows. **QUALIFY** for filtering windowed results. Master these and you can handle 90% of transformation needs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Write **incremental** models (process only new data) instead of full-refresh when tables grow large. Use **materialised views** or **incremental dbt models** for this. Favour readability over cleverness — the next engineer maintaining your SQL will thank you.

t-spark.label: Apache Spark
t-spark.desc: Distributed data processing at scale — batch and streaming with DataFrames, SQL, and structured APIs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Spark** is a distributed compute engine that processes large datasets across a cluster. Write transformations in SQL, Python (PySpark), or Scala; Spark parallelises and optimises execution.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Core abstraction: **DataFrames** (typed, optimised tables). Spark SQL lets you mix SQL with programmatic logic. The **Catalyst optimiser** and **Tungsten execution engine** handle query planning. **Structured Streaming** unifies batch and stream APIs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use Spark for transformations too complex or large for warehouse SQL (custom UDFs, ML feature pipelines, multi-source joins at petabyte scale). Run on managed services (Databricks, EMR, Dataproc) to avoid cluster ops. For smaller datasets (<100 GB), **DuckDB** or warehouse SQL is often faster and cheaper than spinning up Spark.

t-dbt.label: dbt (data build tool)
t-dbt.desc: SQL-first transformation framework — version-controlled models, testing, documentation, and lineage.

links.title: How we structure our dbt projects

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **dbt** lets you write transformations as SQL SELECT statements, organised into models. It handles dependencies, materialisation (table/view/incremental), and builds a DAG of your entire transformation layer.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Models** are SQL files that dbt materialises. **Tests** validate data quality (unique, not_null, relationships, custom). **Documentation** is generated automatically. **Macros** (Jinja) add reusable logic. **Packages** share code across projects.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: dbt is the standard transformation layer in modern data stacks. Structure projects in **staging → intermediate → marts** layers. Run in CI (test on PR, deploy on merge). Use **dbt Cloud** or orchestrate with Airflow/Dagster. Incremental models are essential for large tables — but add complexity; start with full-refresh and optimise when needed.

t-streaming-processing.label: Stream Processing
t-streaming-processing.desc: Transforming data in flight — Flink, Kafka Streams, and real-time computation patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Stream processing transforms events **continuously** as they flow through the system. Use cases: real-time aggregations, fraud detection, feature computation, and alerting.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Apache Flink** is the most capable engine: event-time processing, exactly-once state, complex windowing. **Kafka Streams** is simpler (library, not cluster) for Kafka-native transforms. Key concepts: **windowing** (tumbling, sliding, session), **watermarks** (handling late data), **state management**.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Stream processing is powerful but operationally demanding: stateful checkpointing, backpressure handling, and exactly-once semantics are hard. Use managed **Flink 2.2** (AWS Managed Flink, Confluent Cloud) to reduce ops burden. For many analytics use cases, micro-batch (Spark Structured Streaming, 1–15 min intervals) is 'real-time enough' and far simpler.

t-orchestration.label: Workflow Orchestration
t-orchestration.desc: Scheduling, coordinating, and monitoring data pipelines — Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, and modern alternatives.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **orchestrator** defines pipelines as DAGs (directed acyclic graphs) of tasks, schedules them, manages dependencies, retries failures, and provides visibility into runs.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Airflow**: the incumbent (huge community, operator ecosystem, but complex). **Dagster**: asset-centric (define what data you want, not just tasks), strong dev experience, built-in lineage. **Prefect**: Pythonic, dynamic workflows, cloud-native. All support dbt, Spark, and custom Python.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, **Dagster** is gaining share for new projects (asset-oriented thinking matches how data teams work). Airflow dominates at scale with mature ops teams. Key: treat orchestration code as software — version control, testing, CI/CD. Separate orchestration logic from transformation logic (orchestrate dbt, don't embed SQL in DAG code).

t-data-quality.label: Data Quality & Testing
t-data-quality.desc: Ensuring data is accurate, complete, fresh, and consistent — automated checks, contracts, and anomaly detection.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data quality means your data is **accurate, complete, timely, and consistent**. Bad data leads to bad decisions. Automate checks: null rates, uniqueness, value ranges, freshness, and row counts.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Tools: **dbt tests** (schema + custom), **Great Expectations** (rich assertion library), **Soda** (monitoring + alerting), **Monte Carlo / Elementary** (anomaly detection). **Data contracts** formalise agreements between producers and consumers on schema and quality.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Shift quality left: validate at ingestion (reject bad records early), test in transformation (dbt tests in CI), and monitor in production (anomaly alerts on freshness, volume, distribution). Data contracts between teams prevent surprise breakages. Quality is not a one-time check — it's continuous observability.

t-data-lineage.label: Data Lineage & Cataloging
t-data-lineage.desc: Tracking where data comes from, how it transforms, and where it goes — discoverability and impact analysis.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Lineage** answers: where did this data come from, what transformations touched it, and what downstream depends on it? Essential for debugging, impact analysis, and compliance.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Column-level lineage** traces individual fields through transformations. **Data catalogs** (DataHub, Atlan, Unity Catalog) provide search, documentation, ownership, and access control. **OpenLineage** is the open standard for emitting lineage events from any tool.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Lineage enables confident refactoring (know what breaks before you change), regulatory compliance (GDPR data flow tracking), and root-cause analysis (trace a bad metric back to a broken source). Invest in a catalog early — discovery gets exponentially harder as the data platform grows.

t-data-governance.label: Data Governance & Security
t-data-governance.desc: Access control, privacy, compliance, and organisational policies for managing data responsibly.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data governance defines **who can access what data**, how sensitive data is handled, and how the organisation stays compliant (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2). Without it, data platforms become liability, not asset.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **RBAC/ABAC** for fine-grained access control (table, column, row level). **PII handling**: masking, tokenisation, or encryption. **Retention policies**: auto-delete data past its useful life. **Audit logging**: who accessed what, when.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use a unified catalog (Unity Catalog, Polaris, Lake Formation) as the single governance layer across all engines. Tag columns with sensitivity levels; automate PII detection. Governance shouldn't slow teams down — design it as self-service with guardrails, not gatekeeping with tickets.

t-olap.label: OLAP & Analytical Serving
t-olap.desc: Serving analytical queries at interactive speed — cubes, materialised views, and real-time OLAP engines.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **OLAP** (Online Analytical Processing) engines serve pre-aggregated or columnar data for fast, interactive dashboards and reports. They're optimised for read-heavy analytical queries over large datasets.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Traditional warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) handle most BI. For sub-second latency on high-concurrency dashboards, use **real-time OLAP**: **ClickHouse** (fast columnar), **Apache Druid** (time-series analytics), **Apache Pinot** (user-facing analytics). **Materialised views** pre-compute expensive aggregations.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use your warehouse for internal BI and ad-hoc analysis. Add a real-time OLAP engine only when you need sub-second queries at thousands of QPS (customer-facing analytics, real-time dashboards). ClickHouse has emerged as the go-to for speed and cost. Feed it from Kafka or batch loads from the lake.

t-feature-stores.label: Feature Stores
t-feature-stores.desc: Serving ML features consistently for training and inference — the bridge between data engineering and ML.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **feature store** manages ML features (derived data inputs) so training and serving use the **same features, computed the same way**. Prevents training-serving skew — the #1 silent ML failure mode.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Offline store** (lake/warehouse): batch features for training. **Online store** (Redis, DynamoDB): low-latency features for real-time inference. Feature pipelines compute and push features to both stores. Registry tracks definitions, ownership, and lineage.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Feature stores matter most when: many models share features, features are complex to compute, or real-time serving is required. **Feast** (open-source) is the starting point; **Tecton** and **Databricks Feature Store** offer managed solutions. For simpler setups, a well-governed dbt layer + a cache can suffice.

t-reverse-etl.label: Reverse ETL
t-reverse-etl.desc: Pushing transformed data from the warehouse back into operational tools — CRM, marketing, product.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Reverse ETL** syncs data from your warehouse back to business tools (Salesforce, HubSpot, Braze, Intercom). The warehouse becomes the single source of truth that activates data in operational systems.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Define **audiences and syncs** in the warehouse (SQL models), then tools like **Census**, **Hightouch**, or **dbt + webhooks** push them to destinations. Handles incremental diffs, retries, and schema mapping.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Reverse ETL closes the data loop: ingest → transform → analyse → **activate**. Use it for lead scoring in CRM, personalisation in product, and audience syncs to ad platforms. The warehouse-as-hub pattern means all teams benefit from engineering's cleaned, modeled data without building their own pipelines.

t-semantic-layer.label: Semantic Layer & Metrics
t-semantic-layer.desc: A single source of truth for business metrics — consistent definitions regardless of which tool queries them.

links.title: The Rise of the Semantic Layer (a]s)

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **semantic layer** defines business metrics (revenue, churn, DAU) in one place. Any tool — BI dashboard, notebook, or application — queries through it and gets the **same number**, eliminating 'which number is right?' debates.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Define **metrics** (measures + dimensions + filters) as code. Tools: **dbt Semantic Layer (MetricFlow)**, **Cube**, **Looker's LookML**. The semantic layer translates metric requests into optimised SQL against the warehouse.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Without a semantic layer, every team re-derives metrics differently (slightly different filters, join logic, time zones). The semantic layer is organisational infrastructure: it scales trust in data as the number of consumers grows. In 2026, **dbt Semantic Layer** is gaining traction as the open standard; Cube excels for embedded/customer-facing analytics.

l-compliance-de.desc: GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2 requirements for data platforms.

l-materialised-views.desc: Pre-computed query results refreshed on schedule.

l-realtime-olap.label: Real-time OLAP Engines
l-realtime-olap.desc: ClickHouse, Druid, Pinot for sub-second analytics.

l-offline-store.label: Offline Store
l-offline-store.desc: Batch feature storage for ML training.

l-online-store.label: Online Store
